# PR #8 — Lineage Groups: Phase 0 scaffold + Phase 1 crypto feasibility (GO)

source: https://github.com/croftc/SecurityPolicy/pull/8

captured: 2026-06-15 (via cpettet_croftc). State at capture: OPEN. 40 files, +6158/-3.

branch: claude/lineage-groups-validation-gkmgvj → main

> The Rust execution of the lineage-groups thesis (THESIS.md in discovery) against real
> openmls 0.8.1. The make-or-break Phase 1 crypto gate (E1.1–E1.4) PASSED. Phase findings
> for 0/1/2/2.5/2.6/3 are in this directory. Companion AI coding transcript:
> CODING-TRANSCRIPT.md. Excluded from import (SecurityPolicy plumbing): .github/, renovate.json.

---

## PR description

## Summary

Executes the first two phases of the **Lineage Groups** validation plan under `experiments/lineage-groups/`. Work is sequenced and gated exactly as the plan requires (§6.2): Phase 0 scaffold, then Phase 1 (the crypto feasibility gate) in full, **stopping at the Phase 1 go/no-go gate** — which the plan calls "the single most important result." Phases 2–3 are explicitly conditional on this gate and are not pre-built.

## Phase 1 gate result: **GO** ✅

The make-or-break question (plan §1.4, §7) — *can openmls express "pick a survivor epoch and re-key the other side, or mint a third" with post-compromise security intact?* — is **yes**, verified against the **real `openmls 0.8.1`** library (not docs, per §6.4).

| Exp | Proves | Invariant | Result |
|-----|--------|-----------|--------|
| E1.1 | Removed member can't decrypt post-removal traffic | I4 (PCS) | PASS |
| E1.2 | External-commit join → both sides derive identical group secret | survivor re-key | PASS |
| E1.3 | Fresh genesis → clean third epoch, all converge | "mint a third" | PASS |
| E1.4 | Queued revocation rekeys an offline peer on later apply | broker-carries-revocation | PASS |

Phase 0 gate also green (reproducible trivial scenario under the harness).

## What's here

- **Workspace** with six crates: `lineage-core` (deterministic clock/RNG + ID types), `lineage-mls` (thin openmls wrapper — Phase 1 lives here), `lineage-history` (Phase 2 scaffold), `lineage-sim` (scenario/invariant harness), `lineage-iroh` (Phase 3 stub, depends on nothing — transport kept off the critical path).
- Determinism enforced in logic crates (Lamport clock + seeded ChaCha20; no `SystemTime`/unseeded rand).
- Dependencies pinned exactly (`=x.y.z`) with committed `Cargo.lock`.
- [`PHASE_1_FINDINGS.md`](https://github.com/croftc/SecurityPolicy/blob/claude/lineage-groups-validation-gkmgvj/experiments/lineage-groups/PHASE_1_FINDINGS.md) with full results.

## Corrected thesis assumptions (caught against the real library)

1. **No high-level reinit-with-continuity** in openmls 0.8.1 — only the `ReInitProposal` primitive. "Fresh genesis" is implemented as new-group + re-add; the lineage binding belongs to the Phase 2 governance layer. We compose with MLS, we don't extend it (matches the thesis honesty boundary §1.4).
2. `MlsMessageIn::into_verifiable_group_info` / `into_protocol_message` are test-gated → production must use `.extract()`.
3. Welcomes don't embed the ratchet tree by default → enabled `use_ratchet_tree_extension(true)`.
4. **MLS key material is not bit-reproducible** (`RustCrypto` has no seedable RNG constructor). Reproducibility is scoped to the logic layer; a seedable provider is flagged as a Phase 2 task.

Also noted for Phase 2: openmls 0.8.1 ships a `fork_resolution` module worth evaluating against the governance-tree design.

## Testing

```
cd experiments/lineage-groups
cargo test                  # whole suite (Phase 0 + Phase 1) — all green
cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets   # clean
```

## Scope / not included

No Phase 2 data model (governance fork/heal, deterministic survivor *selection*, conflict hard-stop), history backfill, transport, scale, or security audit — all explicitly gated behind this result (plan §7).

https://claude.ai/code/session_01Vh1uYz3c7xgzBU2sZUZ1jm

## Summary by CodeRabbit

## Release Notes

* **New Features**
  * Added a Rust “Lineage Groups” validation workspace spanning Phase 1–3, including an OpenMLS wrapper, governance operation log, conflict detection/escalation, lineage DAG, deterministic survivor selection, verified in-memory history, and Phase 3 brokered node/transport wiring.
* **Documentation**
  * Added Phase 1–3 findings plus a plan README describing reproducibility and the transport/gating approach.
* **Tests**
  * Added Phase 0 trivial, Phase 1 crypto feasibility, Phase 2 governance/history, and Phase 3 end-to-end broker/network simulation tests.
* **Chores**
  * Updated experiment ignores for Rust backup files and build output.
<!-- end of auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

<details>
<summary>📝 Walkthrough</summary>

## Walkthrough

Completes the experiments/lineage-groups workspace across all phases (0–3): adds deterministic governance operation log with signed, threshold-enforced add/remove/leave/dissolve/fork/recombine; genesis-anchored lineage DAG tracking branch ancestry and standing membership; deterministic conflict detection and survivor selection; per-branch message history with signature verification and backfill; comprehensive openmls 0.8.1 wrapper with Device API; Phase 1 crypto feasibility tests (E1.1–E1.4); Phase 2 governance and history invariant tests (E2.1–E2.8); Phase 3 transport abstraction, blind broker with audit/offline/snapshot support, Node wiring MLS through broker, and end-to-end phase 3 tests (E3.1–E3.4 plus lineage demo).

## Changes

**Lineage Groups Workspace (Phase 0–3)**

| Layer / File(s) | Summary |
|---|---|
| **Workspace manifest, .gitignore, README, PHASE findings** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/.gitignore`, `experiments/lineage-groups/Cargo.toml`, `experiments/lineage-groups/README.md`, `experiments/lineage-groups/PHASE_1_FINDINGS.md`, `experiments/lineage-groups/PHASE_2_FINDINGS.md`, `experiments/lineage-groups/PHASE_3_FINDINGS.md` | Workspace Cargo.toml pins five member crates (lineage-core, lineage-mls, lineage-history, lineage-sim, lineage-iroh), resolver v2, centralizes exact dependency versions (openmls 0.8.1, rand_chacha, ed25519-dalek, serde, sha2, hex, thiserror), sets test profile opt-level 1 for crypto speed. .gitignore covers `/target` and `*.rs.bk`. README documents phases 0–3 status (all GO), crate roles, test commands, and conventions (determinism via Lamport clock + DetRng; pinned deps; verification against real libraries; integration-test naming aligned to phases). PHASE_1_FINDINGS records GO gate with E1.1–E1.4 crypto feasibility tests, confirmed/corrected OpenMLS API assumptions (fresh-genesis via new-group+re-add; ratchet-tree extension for welcomes; CannotDecryptOwnMessage), and reproducibility boundary (logic-layer only; MLS ciphertext not bit-reproducible due to RustCrypto seeding). PHASE_2_FINDINGS records GO gate with E2.1–E2.8 governance/history invariants (all PASS), design decisions (governance reproducibility, genesis immutability, deterministic survivor tiebreaking, escalation-only conflict reconciliation, non-interleaving history branches), scope clarification. PHASE_3_FINDINGS records GO gate with E3.1–E3.4 + lineage demo, explicit negative decision on vendoring real iroh, transport seam approach (broker::BlindBroker + transport module), design/honesty boundaries around blindness/routing metadata/governance carriage/device-loss recovery, and what the experiment establishes vs does not. |
| **lineage-core: Lamport clock, identity types, DetRng, module structure** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/Cargo.toml`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/src/clock.rs`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/src/ids.rs`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/src/rng.rs`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/src/lib.rs` | LamportClock with tick() (counter +1) and observe(remote) (max+1 merge), both panic on u64 overflow. Lamport(u64) timestamp. DeviceId/Did as string-wrapped identities; Did::credential_identity() returns UTF-8 bytes; Did::redacted() produces shortened log-safe hash. GenesisId([u8; 32]) derives SHA-256; to_hex() renders 64-char; Display prints 16 chars. DetRng wraps ChaCha20Rng: from_seed(u64), next_u64(), index(len), Fisher–Yates shuffle(). Crate root declares modules (clock, conflict, dag, gov, ids, keys, rng, survivor) and re-exports primary types. Unit tests verify monotonic ticking, observe correctness, seed determinism, shuffle reproducibility. |
| **lineage-core: signed governance operation log** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/src/gov.rs`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/src/keys.rs` | OpKind (Add/Remove/Leave/Dissolve/Fork/Recombine) with stable byte tags. GenesisRules (admin DIDs, per-kind thresholds); Genesis computes SHA-256 ID over rules+founders. OpBody (genesis, seq, prev hash, kind, subject); signing_bytes() and id() hashing for chaining. SignedOp (body+signatures map). sign_op() assembles chained body, signs per signer. RejectReason enum covers genesis/chain/standing/signature/threshold/subject/dissolution failures. Directory maintains DID→VerifyingIdentity with signature verification. GroupState validates all checks, applies membership mutations. Sig wraps 64-byte Ed25519 with hex serde. SigningIdentity deterministically derives key from (did, seed) via SHA-256; signs. VerifyingIdentity verifies Sig (returns bool). Tests cover deterministic signing, seed equality, verification, forge rejection. |
| **lineage-core: lineage DAG and membership standing** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/src/dag.rs` | BranchNode holds id, parents, ever_members. Lineage manages nodes keyed by genesis ID. add_root() (no parents), fork() (single parent), recombine() (two parents). record_member() inserts into ever_members. ancestors() (BFS), roots() (reachable genesis with no parents), shares_lineage(a, b) (common root check), standing(actor, branch) (actor ever-appeared in any branch sharing roots). All derived from recorded structure, not assertions. |
| **lineage-core: conflict detection and survivor selection** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/src/conflict.rs`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/src/survivor.rs` | detect() computes booted members, flags RemovedThenIncluded contradictions, returns Heal or HardStop. Escalator trait defines on_conflict() hook. reconcile() calls detect, routes hard-stops through escalator, returns resolution unchanged (no auto re-admit). select_survivor(a, b, rule): member count (higher wins); ties broken by byte-lexicographic genesis hash (symmetric). Tests verify precedence, symmetry, order-independence. |
| **lineage-mls: Device API wrapping openmls** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-mls/Cargo.toml`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-mls/src/lib.rs` | Device binds DID-backed credential to MLS state. create_group(), add/remove (return commit/welcome), stage_remove/merge_own_pending. join_from_welcome() (optional ratchet tree), external_commit_join(), fresh_genesis() (reinit+re-add for epoch reset). send() (application message), recv() (decrypt or merge commit). Query: epoch, member_count, epoch_proof, leaf_index_of(did). Wire helpers enforce tls_codec round-trip with exact-deserialize. |
| **Phase 1 crypto feasibility tests** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-mls/tests/e1_crypto_feasibility.rs` | E1.1: PCS forward secrecy (removed cannot decrypt post-removal). E1.2: external-commit convergence (matching epoch secrets, message exchange). E1.3: fresh-genesis independence (third distinct from parents, all converge). E1.4: offline queued revocation (staged remove, offline peer rekeys, removed cannot decrypt). |
| **lineage-sim: deterministic harness and Phase 0 tests** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-sim/Cargo.toml`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-sim/src/lib.rs`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-sim/tests/phase0_trivial.rs` | Scenario owns Lamport clock, DetRng, named Device map, Envelope bus. enqueue() ticks clock. deliver_all() (FIFO), deliver_all_shuffled() (RNG-shuffled). assert_converged() (epoch proof match), logical_digest() (reproducible state hash). Phase 0: ciphertext production, member_count/epoch initialization, digest reproducibility from fixed seed. |
| **lineage-history: per-branch message history and backfill** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-history/Cargo.toml`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-history/src/lib.rs` | Message (author, seq, branch, payload, sig); signing_bytes() helper. BranchHistory (append-only, fold/unfold hides visibility). BackfillError (ForeignGenesis, BadSignature, AlreadyPresent). HistoryStore (branches keyed by GenesisId); backfill_import() validates lineage/genesis sharing, rejects duplicates, verifies all donor signatures, inserts as separate branch without reordering. |
| **Phase 2 governance tests** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/tests/e2_governance.rs` | E2.1: under-threshold rejection. E2.2: genesis admin immutability, forged-op rejection. E2.3: deterministic/symmetric survivor selection with heal. E2.4: hard-stop conflict escalation (custom Escalator). E2.5: rejected merge resting-state (both valid, standing retained). Fuzz: select_survivor symmetry. |
| **Phase 2 history tests** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-history/tests/e2_history.rs` | i9: fold lossless/inert. e2_6: fresh-genesis inherits parent logs unreordered. e2_7: backfill verification and rejection (foreign/tampered). e2_8: no timestamp interleaving across branches. |
| **Phase 3 transport seam and broker** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/Cargo.toml`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/src/lib.rs`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/src/transport.rs`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/src/broker.rs` | Transport abstraction (NodeId/GroupTopic/EnvKind/Envelope). BlindBroker manages per-node queues, per-topic snapshot storage, offline set, audit log. relay() enqueues and audits (cloning ciphertext). set_offline/drain() controls liveness. put_snapshot/get_snapshot() stores/retrieves opaque blobs. never_saw_plaintext() verifies broker never saw provided plaintext in stored ciphertext/snapshot bytes. |
| **Phase 3 node integration and end-to-end tests** <br> `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/src/node.rs`, `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/tests/e3_end_to_end.rs` | Node bridges MLS Device with broker. found_group() (genesis), add_member() (MLS add, ship welcome+handshakes), remove_member() (MLS remove, ship commit), broadcast() (encrypt once, ship to peers), pump() (drain broker mail, route by kind), send_raw/try_recv (test helpers), publish_snapshot/join_via_broker() (snapshot-based recovery). E3.1: partition/reconnect. E3.4: blind broker property. E3.3: device-loss recovery. E3.2: conflict hard-stop. Lineage demo: genesis fork/recombine with provenance tracking. |

## Estimated code review effort

🎯 4 (Complex) | ⏱️ ~90 minutes

- croftc/infosec

### Review — coderabbitai — COMMENTED — 2026-06-14T12:19:15Z

**Actionable comments posted: 4**

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for all review comments with AI agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the
rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate.

Inline comments:
In `@experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/src/clock.rs`:
- Around line 27-35: The tick and observe methods in the Lamport clock struct
use unchecked arithmetic operations (+ 1) that can overflow if the counter
reaches u64::MAX, breaking the monotonic ordering guarantee. Replace the
unchecked additions in both the tick method (where self.counter += 1) and the
observe method (where self.counter.max(remote.0) + 1 is computed) with
checked_add operations. Handle the potential overflow by either returning a
Result type to propagate the error, or panic with a descriptive message to fail
fast rather than silently wrapping the counter value.

In `@experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-mls/src/lib.rs`:
- Around line 336-338: Replace the call to `tls_deserialize()` with
`tls_deserialize_exact()` in the `from_wire` function to ensure the entire
buffer is consumed and untrusted inputs cannot carry unvalidated trailing data
after a valid message prefix. Apply the same change at line 347 where this
deserialization issue also occurs. The `tls_deserialize_exact()` method
validates that deserialization fully consumes the provided buffer, preventing
potential security issues from partial parsing.

In `@experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-sim/src/lib.rs`:
- Around line 43-46: The add_device method silently overwrites existing devices
because it ignores the return value of the insert operation on self.devices.
Before inserting the new Device into self.devices, check if the name key already
exists in self.devices using a contains_key check or similar approach. If the
name already exists, return an error (likely MlsError) to reject the duplicate
rather than allowing the insert to overwrite the existing Device. Only proceed
with the insertion if the name does not already exist.
- Around line 49-55: The device lookup methods `device()` and `device_mut()` use
`.expect()` which panics on unknown device names, but these names are
caller-controlled inputs that should be validated and return errors instead.
Refactor the `device()` method to return `Result<&Device, _>` and `device_mut()`
to return `Result<&mut Device, _>` instead of panicking. Then update all callers
of these methods (including `enqueue()` at lines 58-61 and the delivery logic at
line 83) to handle the `Result` and propagate errors appropriately, ensuring the
methods maintain their `Result` contract for unknown `name` or `to` inputs
rather than crashing the process.
```

</details>

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment by CodeRabbit for review status -->

---

### Review — coderabbitai — COMMENTED — 2026-06-14T14:37:20Z

**Actionable comments posted: 2**

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for all review comments with AI agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the
rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate.

Inline comments:
In `@experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/src/broker.rs`:
- Around line 30-74: The queues field is currently keyed only by NodeId, causing
envelopes from different topics destined to the same node to be stored in the
same queue. This allows drain to return mixed-topic envelopes in a single call,
leading to cross-group envelope loss when one topic's decryption fails and
consumes the entire queue. Change the queues field from BTreeMap<NodeId,
VecDeque<Envelope>> to BTreeMap<(NodeId, GroupTopic), VecDeque<Envelope>>, then
update the relay method to insert using the compound key (env.to.clone(),
env.topic) instead of just env.to.clone(), and update the drain method signature
to accept both a topic parameter and use (to.clone(), topic) as the lookup key
to retrieve only envelopes for that specific node-topic pair.

In `@experiments/lineage-groups/README.md`:
- Around line 21-27: The documentation currently leads with "real MLS +
governance" before explaining that transport lives behind an in-process broker,
which may mislead readers into thinking real P2P was exercised. Restructure the
paragraph to mention the in-process broker and transport trait limitation
earlier in the explanation, before discussing the real MLS and governance
components. This ensures readers understand the actual scope of testing
(deterministic simulation with a broker) before being presented with details
about the real components being wired together.
```

</details>

### cpettet_croftc on `experiments/lineage-groups/Cargo.lock` :L529 — 2026-06-14T12:16:43Z

#cycode_ignore_non_permissive_license_use MPL-2.0 is file-level (weak) copyleft. `hpke-rs` is an upstream dependency of `openmls` used unmodified — no MPL-covered source is modified or redistributed, so no copyleft obligation attaches to CroftC code. HPKE is mandatory for MLS (RFC 9420) and has no permissive-licensed Rust implementation; every openmls crypto provider routes through it. This is internal/experimental validation code under experiments/lineage-groups. Approved for this use case by the repository owner.

---
_Generated by [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)_

---

### cpettet_croftc on `experiments/lineage-groups/Cargo.lock` :L1215 — 2026-06-14T12:16:53Z

#cycode_ignore_non_permissive_license_use `r-efi` is tri-licensed `MIT OR Apache-2.0 OR LGPL-2.1-or-later`; a permissive license (MIT/Apache-2.0) is selected, so no copyleft applies. It is a transitive, build/target-only dependency (via getrandom) and is not linked on the Linux test target used here. Internal/experimental validation code under experiments/lineage-groups. Approved for this use case by the repository owner.

### coderabbitai[bot] on `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/src/clock.rs` :L48 — 2026-06-14T12:19:14Z

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ | _⚡ Quick win_

**Prevent Lamport overflow from breaking monotonic ordering.**

At Line 28 and Line 34, `u64 + 1` is unchecked. If `counter` or input parameter `remote.0` reaches `u64::MAX`, the clock can wrap and violate ordering guarantees. Fail fast with `checked_add` (or return `Result`).

<details>
<summary>Suggested fix</summary>

```diff
 pub fn tick(&mut self) -> Lamport {
-    self.counter += 1;
+    self.counter = self
+        .counter
+        .checked_add(1)
+        .expect("Lamport clock overflow in tick()");
     Lamport(self.counter)
 }

 /// Merge a remote timestamp (on receive): clock = max(local, remote) + 1.
 pub fn observe(&mut self, remote: Lamport) -> Lamport {
-    self.counter = self.counter.max(remote.0) + 1;
+    self.counter = self
+        .counter
+        .max(remote.0)
+        .checked_add(1)
+        .expect("Lamport clock overflow in observe()");
     Lamport(self.counter)
 }
```
</details>

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the
rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate.

In `@experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-core/src/clock.rs` around lines 27
- 35, The tick and observe methods in the Lamport clock struct use unchecked
arithmetic operations (+ 1) that can overflow if the counter reaches u64::MAX,
breaking the monotonic ordering guarantee. Replace the unchecked additions in
both the tick method (where self.counter += 1) and the observe method (where
self.counter.max(remote.0) + 1 is computed) with checked_add operations. Handle
the potential overflow by either returning a Result type to propagate the error,
or panic with a descriptive message to fail fast rather than silently wrapping
the counter value.
```

</details>

<!-- fingerprinting:phantom:poseidon:hawk -->

<!-- cr-comment:v1:685c20128c3b3a67ae946512 -->

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment by CodeRabbit -->

✅ Addressed in commit f71bf33

---

### coderabbitai[bot] on `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-mls/src/lib.rs` — 2026-06-14T12:19:14Z

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_

<details>
<summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>

🌐 Web query:

`In tls_codec used with OpenMLS 0.8.1, does `TlsDeserialize::tls_deserialize(&mut &[u8])` intentionally allow trailing bytes? Is there an official "deserialize exact / consume all bytes" pattern recommended by the library?`

💡 Result:

Yes, the `tls_codec` library used by OpenMLS intentionally allows trailing bytes when using the standard `TlsDeserialize::tls_deserialize` method [1]. This behavior occurs because `TlsDeserialize::tls_deserialize` operates on a `std::io::Read` trait object (or a mutable reference to a reader, such as `&mut &[u8]`) [1]. The reader-based deserializer consumes only the exact number of bytes required to construct the requested type and leaves the internal cursor of the reader at the point where the deserialization finished, leaving any trailing data available in the underlying buffer [1]. For scenarios where you need to ensure that all bytes are consumed and trailing data is treated as an error, the library provides official "exact" deserialization patterns [2][1]: 1. For reader-based deserialization (std environments), use `TlsDeserialize::tls_deserialize_exact(bytes)` [1]. 2. For byte-slice-based deserialization (no_std or std), use `DeserializeBytes::tls_deserialize_exact_bytes(bytes)` [2]. These "exact" methods are designed to validate that the entire input buffer has been consumed; they will return an error if any bytes remain after the structure has been fully deserialized [2][1]. Top results: [2][1][3]

Citations:

- 1: https://docs.rs/tls_codec/latest/tls_codec/trait.Deserialize.html
- 2: https://docs.rs/tls_codec/latest/tls_codec/trait.DeserializeBytes.html
- 3: https://docs.rs/tls_codec/latest/tls_codec/

---

```shell
#!/bin/bash
# Check if the file exists and examine the specified lines
cd experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-mls
head -350 src/lib.rs | tail -30
```

cat Cargo.toml | grep -A 5 -B 5 "tls_codec\|openmls"
```

rg "tls_deserialize" -n src/lib.rs

cat Cargo.toml 2>/dev/null | grep -A 20 "\[workspace"
```

---

🏁 Script executed:

```shell
#!/bin/bash
# Check what types MlsMessageIn and RatchetTreeIn are (likely from openmls)
cd experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-mls
rg "MlsMessageIn|RatchetTreeIn" src/lib.rs | head -20
```

Repository: croftc/SecurityPolicy

Length of output: 285

---

🌐 Web query:

`OpenMLS 0.8.1 MlsMessageIn RatchetTreeIn tls_deserialize_exact API support`

💡 Result:

In OpenMLS 0.8.1, the `tls_deserialize_exact` API is a supported and standard method for deserializing incoming messages [1][2][3]. It is primarily used to deserialize byte slices into an `MlsMessageIn` struct [1][4], which represents messages received from a delivery service [5][4]. Regarding the specific components mentioned: 1. MlsMessageIn: The `MlsMessageIn` struct is the primary entry point for incoming messages in OpenMLS [1][6]. It is designed to be deserialized from bytes using `MlsMessageIn::tls_deserialize_exact(bytes)` [1][2][3]. This method ensures that the entire provided byte slice is consumed and returns an error if the message is malformed [1][2]. 2. RatchetTreeIn: While `MlsMessageIn` handles the message framing, Ratchet Tree structures are managed within the `TreeSync` module [7]. As of version 0.8.1, the `TreeSync` module has been updated to expose new public accessors that allow developers to inspect the state of the ratchet tree, including access to blank leaves and parent nodes [8][9][7]. 3. tls_deserialize_exact: This is an active API available for various OpenMLS types (including `MlsMessageIn`) when the `std` crate feature is enabled [10]. It is the recommended approach for deserializing network-received data to ensure all bytes are accounted for [1][2][10]. OpenMLS 0.8.1 was released on 2026-02-13, primarily focused on dependency updates and exposing new accessors for ratchet tree inspection [8][7].

Citations:

- 1: https://book.openmls.tech/user_manual/processing.html
- 2: https://book.openmls.tech/print.html
- 3: https://context7.com/openmls/openmls/llms.txt
- 4: https://www.mintlify.com/openmls/openmls/user-manual/processing-messages
- 5: https://www.mintlify.com/openmls/openmls/api/messages/framing
- 6: https://www.mintlify.com/openmls/openmls/security/message-validation
- 7: https://blog.openmls.tech/posts/2026-03-04-this-month-in-openmls/
- 8: https://github.com/openmls/openmls/releases/tag/openmls-v0.8.1
- 9: https://github.com/openmls/openmls/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md
- 10: https://docs.rs/openmls/latest/openmls/framing/enum.Sender.html

---

🏁 Script executed:

```shell
#!/bin/bash
# Look at how these functions are called to understand data source
cd experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-mls
rg "from_wire|deser_ratchet_tree" src/lib.rs -B 2 -A 2
```

Repository: croftc/SecurityPolicy

Length of output: 1543

---



</details>

**Use `tls_deserialize_exact()` to reject trailing bytes.**

Lines 337 and 347 accept deserialized messages without validating the entire buffer is consumed. Untrusted inputs in `recv()`, `join_from_welcome()`, and `external_commit_join()` can carry valid prefix + unvalidated trailing payload.

OpenMLS 0.8.1 provides `tls_deserialize_exact()` specifically for this:

<details>
<summary>Proposed fix</summary>

```diff
 fn from_wire(bytes: &[u8]) -> Result<MlsMessageIn> {
-    MlsMessageIn::tls_deserialize(&mut &bytes[..])
-        .map_err(|e| MlsError::Wire(format!("{e:?}")))
+    MlsMessageIn::tls_deserialize_exact(bytes)
+        .map_err(|e| MlsError::Wire(format!("{e:?}")))
 }

 fn deser_ratchet_tree(bytes: &[u8]) -> Result<RatchetTreeIn> {
-    RatchetTreeIn::tls_deserialize(&mut &bytes[..])
-        .map_err(|e| MlsError::Wire(format!("{e:?}")))
+    RatchetTreeIn::tls_deserialize_exact(bytes)
+        .map_err(|e| MlsError::Wire(format!("{e:?}")))
 }
```
</details>

Also applies to: 347

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the
rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate.

In `@experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-mls/src/lib.rs` around lines 336 -
338, Replace the call to `tls_deserialize()` with `tls_deserialize_exact()` in
the `from_wire` function to ensure the entire buffer is consumed and untrusted
inputs cannot carry unvalidated trailing data after a valid message prefix.
Apply the same change at line 347 where this deserialization issue also occurs.
The `tls_deserialize_exact()` method validates that deserialization fully
consumes the provided buffer, preventing potential security issues from partial
parsing.
```

</details>

<!-- fingerprinting:phantom:poseidon:hawk -->

✅ Addressed in commit f71bf33

### coderabbitai[bot] on `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-sim/src/lib.rs` — 2026-06-14T12:19:14Z

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ | _⚡ Quick win_

**Duplicate device names silently overwrite prior state.**

`add_device(name, did)` replaces an existing device when `name` already exists (`insert` return value is ignored). A repeated `name` can discard live group state and corrupt scenario outcomes.

Fix: reject duplicate `name` before insertion and return an error instead of replacing the existing `Device`.

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the
rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate.

In `@experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-sim/src/lib.rs` around lines 43 -
46, The add_device method silently overwrites existing devices because it
ignores the return value of the insert operation on self.devices. Before
inserting the new Device into self.devices, check if the name key already exists
in self.devices using a contains_key check or similar approach. If the name
already exists, return an error (likely MlsError) to reject the duplicate rather
than allowing the insert to overwrite the existing Device. Only proceed with the
insertion if the name does not already exist.
```

</details>

<!-- fingerprinting:phantom:poseidon:hawk -->

<!-- cr-comment:v1:9b4077adcbc67a2e70ed4726 -->

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment by CodeRabbit -->

✅ Addressed in commit f71bf33

---

### coderabbitai[bot] on `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-sim/src/lib.rs` :L79 — 2026-06-14T12:19:14Z

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ | _⚡ Quick win_

**Unknown device input crashes the harness instead of returning an error.**

`name`/`to` are caller-controlled inputs, but lookups use `expect("unknown device")` (including Line 83 in delivery). A typo or stale route panics the process and bypasses the `Result` contract on `deliver_all*`.

Fix: make device lookup fallible and propagate an error (`Result`) for unknown `name`/`to` in `device*`, `enqueue`, and delivery.
   


Also applies to: 58-61, 83-83

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the
rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate.

In `@experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-sim/src/lib.rs` around lines 49 -
55, The device lookup methods `device()` and `device_mut()` use `.expect()`
which panics on unknown device names, but these names are caller-controlled
inputs that should be validated and return errors instead. Refactor the
`device()` method to return `Result<&Device, _>` and `device_mut()` to return
`Result<&mut Device, _>` instead of panicking. Then update all callers of these
methods (including `enqueue()` at lines 58-61 and the delivery logic at line 83)
to handle the `Result` and propagate errors appropriately, ensuring the methods
maintain their `Result` contract for unknown `name` or `to` inputs rather than
crashing the process.
```

</details>

<!-- fingerprinting:phantom:poseidon:hawk -->

<!-- cr-comment:v1:40ad685388ee8b2284969355 -->

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment by CodeRabbit -->

### coderabbitai[bot] on `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/src/broker.rs` — 2026-06-14T14:37:19Z

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ | _🏗️ Heavy lift_

**Route and drain by `(NodeId, GroupTopic)` to prevent cross-group envelope loss.**

Line 30 and Line 66 currently key delivery only by `NodeId`, so `drain(&to)` can hand topic-A and topic-B envelopes to the same pump cycle. With `Node::pump` (Line 136 in `experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/src/node.rs`) this can consume the wrong topic and drop valid queued mail when one decrypt fails.  
At-risk inputs: `Envelope.to`, `Envelope.topic`, and any node ID reused across multiple active groups.

<details>
<summary>Proposed cross-file fix</summary>

```diff
diff --git a/experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/src/broker.rs b/experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/src/broker.rs
@@
-    queues: BTreeMap<NodeId, VecDeque<Envelope>>,
+    queues: BTreeMap<(NodeId, GroupTopic), VecDeque<Envelope>>,
@@
-        self.queues.entry(env.to.clone()).or_default().push_back(env);
+        self.queues
+            .entry((env.to.clone(), env.topic))
+            .or_default()
+            .push_back(env);
@@
-    pub fn drain(&mut self, to: &NodeId) -> Vec<Envelope> {
+    pub fn drain_topic(&mut self, to: &NodeId, topic: GroupTopic) -> Vec<Envelope> {
         if self.offline.contains_key(to) {
             return Vec::new();
         }
         self.queues
-            .get_mut(to)
+            .get_mut(&(to.clone(), topic))
             .map(|q| q.drain(..).collect())
             .unwrap_or_default()
     }
diff --git a/experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/src/node.rs b/experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/src/node.rs
@@
-        for env in broker.drain(&self.id) {
+        for env in broker.drain_topic(&self.id, self.topic) {
             match env.kind {
                 EnvKind::Welcome => {
                     self.dev.join_from_welcome(&env.ciphertext, None)?;
                 }
                 EnvKind::Handshake | EnvKind::App => match self.dev.recv(&env.ciphertext)? {
```
</details>

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the
rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate.

In `@experiments/lineage-groups/crates/lineage-iroh/src/broker.rs` around lines 30
- 74, The queues field is currently keyed only by NodeId, causing envelopes from
different topics destined to the same node to be stored in the same queue. This
allows drain to return mixed-topic envelopes in a single call, leading to
cross-group envelope loss when one topic's decryption fails and consumes the
entire queue. Change the queues field from BTreeMap<NodeId, VecDeque<Envelope>>
to BTreeMap<(NodeId, GroupTopic), VecDeque<Envelope>>, then update the relay
method to insert using the compound key (env.to.clone(), env.topic) instead of
just env.to.clone(), and update the drain method signature to accept both a
topic parameter and use (to.clone(), topic) as the lookup key to retrieve only
envelopes for that specific node-topic pair.
```

</details>

<!-- fingerprinting:phantom:poseidon:hawk -->

✅ Addressed in commits ab6f4b9 to 41fec00

### coderabbitai[bot] on `experiments/lineage-groups/README.md` — 2026-06-14T14:37:20Z

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟡 Minor_

<details>
<summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>

🏁 Script executed:

```shell
#!/bin/bash
# Check if the file exists and read the relevant sections
fd "README.md" experiments/lineage-groups/ && echo "---FILE FOUND---" && cat -n experiments/lineage-groups/README.md | sed -n '15,50p'
```

Repository: croftc/SecurityPolicy

Length of output: 2840

---



</details>

**Clarify what Phase 3 validated.**

Lines 21–27 and 43–46 lead with "real MLS + governance" before explaining transport stays behind a trait with an in-process broker. Reorder to surface the broker earlier so readers don't infer P2P coverage that wasn't exercised.

<details>
<summary>Suggested wording</summary>

```diff
-Phase 3 wires the real Phase 1 MLS + Phase 2 governance end-to-end over a transport seam; the real
-`iroh` dependency is deliberately not vendored ...
+Phase 3 validates Phase 1 MLS + Phase 2 governance end-to-end over a transport seam via the
+in-process blind broker; the real `iroh` dependency is deliberately not vendored ...
```

```diff
-- **`lineage-iroh`** — Phase 3 end-to-end slice: a transport seam, a
-  structurally-**blind broker** ..., and node wiring that runs the real MLS + governance across nodes.
+- **`lineage-iroh`** — Phase 3 transport-seam slice: a structurally-**blind broker** ... and node
+  wiring that exercises MLS + governance across nodes without vendoring real `iroh`.
```
</details>

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against current code. Fix only still-valid issues, skip the
rest with a brief reason, keep changes minimal, and validate.

In `@experiments/lineage-groups/README.md` around lines 21 - 27, The documentation
currently leads with "real MLS + governance" before explaining that transport
lives behind an in-process broker, which may mislead readers into thinking real
P2P was exercised. Restructure the paragraph to mention the in-process broker
and transport trait limitation earlier in the explanation, before discussing the
real MLS and governance components. This ensures readers understand the actual
scope of testing (deterministic simulation with a broker) before being presented
with details about the real components being wired together.
```

</details>
